Jeanne Pritchard

Subject: Western Growers Association -- Agricultural tour for legislators 
On December 8, 1983, I spoke with Miriam Hubbard and Jasper 
Hempel of Western Growers Association about an informational agricul-
tural tour provided to a number of legislative officials by Western 
Growers Association. 
The tour was provided on October 11, 1983, and included air 
transportation for the officials from their districts to the start of 
the tour in Salinas. 
Seven legislators and one AA participated in the tour. 
However, 
two of the legislators provided their own transportation to the star of 
the tour, so the air transportation was provided to only five of the 
legislators and the AA. 
Dan Haley, Western Growers' lobbyist, was liaison for Western 
Growers, and Rusty Areias was liaison for the legislators. 
Haley and 
Areias communicated on a number of occasions concerning the travel 
arrangements. 
Western Growers chartered an airplane to fly three of 
the legislators from Sacramento to Salinas. 
Western Growers used their 
own private plane to fly two of the l~gislators and the AA from Los 
Angeles to Salinas. 
Dan Haley was host to the group on the plane from 
Sacramento, and Jasper Hempel, also one of Western Growers' lobbyists, 
was host to the group on the plane from Los Angeles. 
Although the two lobbyists apparently reliazed that the air 
transportation constituted a gift, they did not realize that their 
activities -- Dan Haley's communications with Rusty Areias, and Dan 
Haley's and Jasper Hempel's hosting the legislators on the plane trips 
constituted "arranging" the gift. 
Mr. Hempel asked what could be done to rectify the violation. 
I 
suggsted that if he requested, and the legislators provided, reimburse-
ment of the cost of the trip, it might minimize the seriousness of the 
violation, although it would not rectify it. 
I also suggested that 
they attach a full explanation and copies of the correspondence to 
their quarterly reports.

On December 8, 1983, I was advised by Ms. Jean Pritchard, 
Political Reform Consultant, 
ir Political Practices Commission, 
that Dan Haley and myself, as registered lobbyists for the Western 
Growers Association, have violated Government Code Section 86203 
and Opinion No. 81-003, 7 FPPC 1 (attached) because we acted as 
intermediaries by helping to arrange transportation for the Salinas 
agricultural tour you participated in on October 11, 1983. 
By 
acting as intermediaries and accompanying you on the plane trip 
without another WGA representative on board, Dan and I apparently 
arranged travel or provided a gift to you in excess of $10 per 
month, which is prohibited by the Act. 
At the time, neither Dan 
nor I was aware that we had committed a technical violation of the 
act. 
Nevertheless, Western Growers Association requests that you 
reimburse Western Growers Association $409.00 which represents your 
pro rata ~ share of the $858.00 airplane cost, minus our $10.00 
gift which is permissible. 
While this reimbursement will not cure 
WGA's technical violation, your reimbursement of that sum may, I 
understand, ameliorate a violation on your part. 
WGA, Dan Haley and I sincerely apologize for this regrettable 
error and for any inconvenience this may cause you. 
If you have 
any questions regarding this matter, please call me at 714/863-1000, 
or Ms. Jean Pritchard of the FPP ,at 916/322-5660.

Enclosed please find FPPC Opinion No. 81-003 (Institute 
for Governmental Advocates, 7 FPPC 1), which discusses the 
concept of "arranging" as it applies·to lobbyists involved in 
making gifts to state officials. 
Although the circumstances addressed by the opinion are 
quite' different from the situation we discussed on the telephone, 
the general discussion of "arranging" will provide guidance in 
defining prohibited activities of a lobbyist in connection with 
making gifts to officials. 
Specifically, it is stated in the 
analysis, on page 5, that: 
A lobbyist is acting as an agent in, or arranging 
for, the making of a gift by another when he or she: 
1. 
Takes any action involving contact with 
a third party which facilitates the making 
of the gift. 
2. 
Has any contact with the public official 
who is to be the recipient of the gift 
which facilitates the making of the gift. 
With regard to the situation we discussed on the telephone, 
please note that Government Code Section 82028(b) (1) excludes 
"informational material" from the definition of "gift"; however, 
that section provides that, "No payment for travel or reimburse-
ment for any expenses shall be deemed 'informational material. '" 
Please call me if you have any questions. 
Sincerely, 
Jeanne Pritchard

On September 27 and October 12 I submitted an oplnlon request on 
the following questions related to the Yuba County Water Agency: 
1. 
Maya director of the Yuba County Water Agency who is also 
a director of a California Water District lawfully act on matters 
which directly affect the water district? 
2. 
Maya director of the Yuba County Water Agency who owns 
land within a California Water District lawfully participate in 
Agency actions which will provide water to such district? 
I have attached copies of our correspondence in this matter. 
You have asked for additional clarification on these questions. 
Pursuant to the authority of §5.l(c) and (d) of the Agency Act (see 
Water Code--Appendix §84-5.1(c) and (d», the Water Agency sells 
water to the member districts. 
In addition the Agency has the power 
to construct water storage, flood control and power generation 
facilities and has done so in the construction of the New Bullards 
Bar Dam. 
In addition the Agency has over the past several years 
planned for the construction of a canal taking water from the Yuba 
River to the southern part of the County for sale to the three 
water districts organized in that area. 
Although recently two of 


The Honorable John K. Van De Kamp 
ATTN: 
JACK R. WINKLER, Assistant Attorney General 
October 18, 1983 
Page 2 
the three districts have apparently withdrawn from the joint 
project, Wheatland Water District is still interested and all 
three are interested in entering into water contracts with the 
Agency. 
The above are the types of "matters which directly affect" the 
Hater District to which my letter of September 27 referred. 
In reference to the second question noted above I have advised 
the Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency on several 
occasions in relation to conflicts of interest and my letter to 
them dated May 16, 1983 was merely a summary of those previous 
opinions. 
The basic provlslon of the Political Reform Act of 1974 provides 
as follows: 
"No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in 
any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows 
or has reason to know he has a financial interest." 
"Public official" as used in this section means "every member, officer, 
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency," 
(Government Code §82048) and "local government agency" would certainly 
include the Yuba County Water Agency (See Government Code §8204l). 
In the present question the member of the Board of Directors is a 
rice grower and owner of substantial property in the Wheatland 
Water District. 
The actions of the Agency in securing a loan for 
a canal system and then selling the water to the district which 
would in turn develop a distribution system and sell the water to 
the individual landowners in the district would be of benefit to 
him in that his costs in securing water would be decreased. 
However 
the cases have concluded that such a public official is not required 
to disqualify himself from participation in such decisions if the 
benefit to him will be the same as that to a significant segment of 
the public (see Metro olitan Water Dist. of Southern California v. 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
7 
CA 
. 
In t is 
instance the Director will receive the same benefit as nearly all 
the other landowners in the Wheatland Water District and therefore 
is not disqualified from participating in the questioned decisions. 
You also raised a question as to the applicability of Government 
Code §ll26 to this question. 
This section as well as Government 
Code §l090 et seq. relate primarily to incompatible employment of 
those in public service. 
This situation is not raised in the pre-
sent fact situation. 
Here the question is whether or not the 
Director is receiving a private benefit from a public decision. 


The Honorable John K. Van De Kamp 
ATTN: 
JACK R. WINKLER, Assistant Attorney General 
October 18, 1983 
Page 3 
In addition §7.l of the Agency Act (see Water Code--Appendix 
§84-7.l) prohibits a director from being "interested in any manner, 
directly or indirectly, in any contract awarded or to be awarded 
by the Board of Directors, or in the profits to be derived therefrom." 
There is no statutory guidance in construing this section and no 
reported cases. 
However there is no indication that the intent of 
this sect 
is more restrictive than the provisions of the Political 
Reform Act of 1974 discussed above. 
It has been and remains the conclusion of this office that a member 
of the Board of Supervisors sitting ex officio as a member of the 
Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency is not disqualified 
from participating in decisions of the Board of Directors in planning 
and constructing water conveyance facilities to provide water to the 
Wheatland Water District by the fact that that Supervisor is also a 
landowner in the district, and would benefit along with others by 
the receipt of such water. 
I appreciate your attention to the above noted questions. 
Very truly

Dear Mr. Van de Kamp: 
In my capacity as General Counsel to the Yuba County Water Agency 
I hereby submit a request for an opinion on the following subject. 
The Yuba County Water Agency was established by Chapter 788 of the 
Statutes of 1959 (see now Chapter 84 of the Water Code--Appendix). 
Section 7 of the original Agency Act provided that the Board of 
Supervisors of Yuba County should serve, ex officio, as the Board 
of Directors of the Agency. 
(Water Code--Appendix §84-7). 
However 
this section was amended by Chapter 719 of the Statutes of 1979 to 
provide that in addition to the Board of Supervisors, the Agency 
Board shall also include two members of the advisory council, one 
to represent the districts north of the Yuba River and one to 
represent the districts south of the Yuba River, in staggered 
two year terms. 
The Advisory Council is made up of officers of the various water 
related districts and the two cities in the County. 
Therefore 
the two additional directors are also officers of such districts. 
The question has arisen whether such an Agency director can act 
on matters which directly effect the district on which-he 
also serves. 
Attached is an opinion of this office dated May 16, 
1983, the first part of which is directed to this question.

On September 27 and October 12 I submitted an opinion request, with 
a clarifying letter sent on October 18, 1983. 
I am now in receipt 
of a copy of a letter to you from Mr. Paul Minasian, the attorney 
for South Yuba Water District, attempting to provide additional 
background information. 
He refers to the PL 984 project and notes the various districts 
that anticipated receipt of such water. 
It should be understood 
that the amount of water to be delivered by the PL 984 project 
was approximately 107,200 acre feet with only 12,200 acre feet 
of that intended for the TTlunicipal end industrie.l uses. 
This 
amount would have a minimal affect on the property owners in the 
districts affected. 
The members of the Board living in Linda, 
Olivehurst and the City of Marysville, although landowners, are 
certainly not affected by a water project in the same way that an 
agricultural landowner would be. 
It should also be noted that if it were a conflict of interest for 
a Director on the Agency Board who is also a director of a member 
district to act on a water contract or project that may limit or 
affect his district's future water rights, the two district repre-
sentatives on the Board would almost never be able to participate 
in an action of the Board. 
Certainly, that was not the intent of 
the legislature in adopting the revision to §7 of the Agency Act.

You have recently apparently received a letter from the 
Yuba County Water Agency making inquiries in regard to your 
opinion in respect to a potential conflict of interest on 
the Yuba County Water Agency Board. 
That request for an 
opinion was initiated at the request of three of the seven 
directors who contacted the County Counsel immediately after 
a vote in which those three directors were overruled by the 
majority of four. 
At the following meeting of the Yuba 
County Water Agency, it became clear that the request had 
been forwarded to the Attorney General's Office at the 
request of the minority. 
At that meeting, on October 11, 
1983, the Board of the Yuba County Water Agency specifically 
asked that the request to the Attorney General be expanded 
to deal with potential conflicts on the part of the other 
members of the Board. 
You should by this time have received 
that request. 
We wish to make it clear that by mentioning and describing 
other Board members in this letter, we are not inferring 
that any conflict of interest should be declared by them 
except in regard to Doug Waltz in certain circumstances. 
To understand the inquiries, you will need some factual 
background and it is our purpose in writing to 
directly 
to provide that factual background. 
Obviously, if there is 
any difference between our understanding and that of Mr. 
Barlow, Yuba County Counsel, Mr. Barlow should have an 
opportunity to correct it but we are taking this initiative 
so that this opinion can be obtained as quickly as possible.

Yuba County Water Agency consists of five members of 
the Board of Supervisors, and two representatives of the 
member units of the Agency who are elected by those member 
units. 
In reviewing Section 7 of the Act you can see the 
procedure and the qualifications required. 
The Advisory 
Counsel has adopted rules and regulations. 
Those rules and 
regulations provide that the only person who may serve upon 
the Advisory Counsel are members of the boards of the various 
member units, and that the only persons who may be elected 
to the Agency Board from the Advisory Counsel shall be 
members of the boards of the various member units. 
Therefore, 
it is a qualification for the two representatives, not 
members of the Board of Supervisors, that they be members of 
the Board of Directors of a member unit, and that they be 
elected by the Advisory Counsel consisting only of members 
of the Board of Directors of member units. 
The particular range of issues in which the conflict of 
interest question arises will be important for you 
to know. 
The Yuba County Water Agency has proposed a water distribution 
project to divert waters from the Yuba River for use within 
the South County Area. 
There are three irrigation districts, 
South Yuba Water District, Wheatland Water District, Brophy 
Water District, and two municipal and industrial water 
users, Olivehurst Public Utility District and Linda County 
Water District, and one city, the City of Wheatland, who 
would be served by this project. 
The project is to be 
financed by a PL984 Federal Loan. 
South Yuba Water District and Brophy Water

regardless of whether or not the PL984 Project is ever 
constructed.

, on page 5, that: 
A lobbyist is acting as an agent in, or arranging 
for, the making of a gift by another when he or she: 
1. 
Takes any action involving contact with 
a third party which facilitates the making 
of the gift. 
2. 
Has any contact with the public official 
who is to be the recipient of the gift 
which facilitates the making of the gift. 
With regard to the situation we discussed on the telephone, 
please note that Government Code Section 82028(b) (1) excludes 
"informational material" from the definition of "gift"; however, 
that section provides that, "No payment for travel or reimburse-
ment for any expenses shall be deemed 'informational material. '"

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Western Growers Association lobbyists violated gift and intermediary provisions of the Political Reform Act by arranging and hosting legislative officials on subsidized air transportation to an agricultural tour.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that the lobbyists violated Government Code Section 86203 by acting as intermediaries to arrange travel gifts exceeding $10 per month to state officials, and recommended reimbursement and disclosure on quarterly reports to ameliorate the violation.

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Jeanne Pritchard Dote 12/29/83 Subject: Western Growers Association -- Agricultural tour for legislators On December 8, 1983, I spoke with Miriam Hubbard and Jasper Hempel of Western Growers Association about an informational agricul- tural tour provided to a number of legislative officials by Western Growers Association. The tour was provided on October 11, 1983, and included air transportation for the officials from their districts to the start of the tour in Salinas. Seven legislators and one AA participated in the tour. However, two of the legislators provided their own transportation to the star of the tour, so the air transportation was provided to only five of the legislators and the AA. Dan Haley, Western Growers' lobbyist, was liaison for Western Growers, and Rusty Areias was liaison for the legislators. Haley and Areias communicated on a number of occasions concerning the travel arrangements. Western Growers chartered an airplane to fly three of the legislators from Sacramento to Salinas. Western Growers used their own private plane to fly two of the l~gislators and the AA from Los Angeles to Salinas. Dan Haley was host to the group on the plane from Sacramento, and Jasper Hempel, also one of Western Growers' lobbyists, was host to the group on the plane from Los Angeles. Although the two lobbyists apparently reliazed that the air transportation constituted a gift, they did not realize that their activities -- Dan Haley's communications with Rusty Areias, and Dan Haley's and Jasper Hempel's hosting the legislators on the plane trips constituted "arranging" the gift. Mr. Hempel asked what could be done to rectify the violation. I suggsted that if he requested, and the legislators provided, reimburse- ment of the cost of the trip, it might minimize the seriousness of the violation, although it would not rectify it. I also suggested that they attach a full explanation and copies of the correspondence to their quarterly reports. p P WESTERN ~l1ER:s/~<lUJfit\L ACTION COMMITTEE ~(J.'I3~ 2130 • NEWPORT BEACH, CA 9265c The Honorable Norm Waters Ms. Susan Reed California State Assembly State Capitol Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Norm: December 21, 1983 On December 8, 1983, I was advised by Ms. Jean Pritchard, Political Reform Consultant, ir Political Practices Commission, that Dan Haley and myself, as registered lobbyists for the Western Growers Association, have violated Government Code Section 86203 and Opinion No. 81-003, 7 FPPC 1 (attached) because we acted as intermediaries by helping to arrange transportation for the Salinas agricultural tour you participated in on October 11, 1983. By acting as intermediaries and accompanying you on the plane trip without another WGA representative on board, Dan and I apparently arranged travel or provided a gift to you in excess of $10 per month, which is prohibited by the Act. At the time, neither Dan nor I was aware that we had committed a technical violation of the act. Nevertheless, Western Growers Association requests that you reimburse Western Growers Association $409.00
embedding.summary: This document package contains FPPC correspondence and enforcement communications regarding a December 1983 incident where Western Growers Association lobbyists arranged subsidized air transportation for seven legislators and one aide to attend an agricultural tour in Salinas on October 11, 1983, constituting prohibited gifts under the Political Reform Act.
